message: The concern is regarding manipulation and obfuscation.  The reason i prefer the term "pro-abortion" is that it is *merely* descriptive and doesnt manipulate.  Obviously, anyone engaged in the abortion debate is aware what side "pro-choice" people fall on; the issue is how that term shifts the focus *away from* a discussion of killing the unborn, and *toward* a discussion of limiting or not limiting someone's choice.  **All** issues of legality are questions of choice (theft is illegal bc we have laws that eliminate the choice to take other people's stuff); **this particular** issue of legality regards the killing of the unborn.
"Pro-abortion" is incredibly misleading. Most pro-choice people don't want people to have abortions, but think that it isn't always as bad as the alternatives, so it should be an option.

message:
There is evidence to suggest that weight is not as changeable as people think in many cases. There is evidence to suggest that any large weight loss will eventually be reversed once the body’s metabolism readjusts. 

The fat acceptance movement is about not devaluing someone’s worth as a human being because of their weight. It’s about revealing a disparity in medical care (someone linked to it above, that fat people are less likely to received adequate care than skinny people). And it’s about taking care of yourself in a way that makes sense to you. 

The fat acceptance movement actually promotes healthy eating and exercise. A common mantra is “moving your body in a way that feels good to you.” Many people gain weight and don’t exercise because the common exercises hurt them in some way, and the fat acceptance movement promotes finding ways to be active that don’t give you pain. The fat acceptance movement also promotes the general idea that as long as you are eating well, exercising, and taking care of yourself, *your weight doesn’t matter*. Weight, and how you look, is not always an appropriate indicator of health.

I understand the choice isn't malicious. 

Theres a big difference between other examples of things babies can't consent to. Circumcision isn't just parents looking out for their child because the decision sticks even when the child is an adult and the parents have no control over them. It is permanent. Parents can't give their child a permanent tattoo because it strips them of the right to choose to not have one as an adult.

Everything, absolutey everything, done to babies is done without their consent and most things have some shade of gray as to if it is a net-good for the baby over the entirety of their life or a net-negative. 

Parents may choose not to eat meat, with efforts to get the required nutrients from other sources because they believe the water-resource intensity issues and morality concerns are more likely to cause problems for the child in life than a lack of eating meat. But some people may disagree. Either way, the child did not consent to that choice but their brains development, growth, and future opportunities may be dependent on it. 

Parents may choose to keep their baby inside all the time, supplementing vitamin D, to ensure the kid does not get exposed to the sun and increase the risk of skin cancer.  Others may believe that experiencing the outdoors is a net positive and outweighs the risk, but sun-exposure over life is cumulative and the child is being impacted by that choice of their parents, they did not consent. 

Parents may choose to teach their baby a specific language. Others may argue a different language is a net-positive for the child given the direction and status of the world. Ie don’t teach your child your native language, teach English. Either way, once the choice is made the child is affected for life. Sure, they can attempt to learn a different language later in life, but their brain has different abilities and capabilities. Once that decision is made to the infant, it affects them for life. 

In this case, assuming the parents considered the potential benefits and risks - even if you disagree with their conclusion - and chose what they thought was best for the child over the entirety of their life, the child’s ability to consent is not any any more relevant than the child’s consent for being vegetarian or a shut-in. 

There are extremes and situations where society deems there is irrefutable evidence that an action is a net-negative on a child (shaking a baby to get them to stop crying, sexual actions) that we absolutey and rightly ban it. That’s based on the nearly universally-agreed understanding that it is a net-negative. It’s not about whether or not the child could consent to the choice.  

Every decision a parent makes for their child does not have their babies consent. 

Circumcision decisions have complexity and parents choosing to do so are not making that choice selfishly or maliciously.

@maximum-plum, @flavorful-fusion, I appreciate the nuanced arguments, but I still can't wrap my head around circumcision. It's a permanent, irreversible decision made without the child's consent. I understand parents making choices based on what they believe is best, but this feels different. It's not like choosing a language or diet, which can be changed later. This is about bodily autonomy, and I struggle with the idea that a child's body can be altered permanently without their consent.

My personal belief on the administration of both hormone blockers and cross-sex hormone treatment is that, generally, they should be highly discouraged in favor of any number of other intensive therapeutic techniques (possibly even before some specific age, different for each sex). 

Hormone blockers carry significant neurocognitive and physiological uncertainties w/r/t development but is far and away the safer option so **comparatively** I don't have an issue with their administration under 16. Cross-sex hormones ought to always remain an absolute last resort and should be restricted only to individuals 1) who have already undergone blockers; OR 2) individuals over some age (18? who knows); OR 3) have actively expressed suicidality in combination with officially diagnosed gender dysphoria.

Again, none of this is relevant to the question of whether it's hypocritical to bar a child under 16 from legally pursuing sex with a non-minor on the grounds of lack of neuropsychological development while simultaneously saying that a child is allowed to (arguably, *must*) initiate and participate in neuropsychological development-altering decisions based on their perception of their brain chemistry.

> instead of passive-aggressively attempting to make me look like a sociopath

I am not attempting to do that. I apologize for giving you that impression.

> neuropsychological development as it relates to a child declaring themself to have improper brain chemistry vs a child declaring their intention to pursue a sexual relationship, vis-a-vis age of consent.

There is a scientific consensus on this that agrees with your view. 16 is accepted as the appropriate age to decide on biologically transitioning genders. It is logical to me, barring extenuating circumstances. I would posit that it is not a catch all age, and there should not be an outright ban on it happening before 16. That there are situations where transitioning before 16 would have greater benefits than risks. The case of the 12 year old is an example of this. They had severe depression and suicide with a known cause of gender identity.

I believe in this case that the suffering and self harm out weigh the risks of the hormone treatment. Do you believe there is a point where suffering out weighs the risks?

My question of blockers v. hormones is relevant as they are different treatments with different goals. The morality of each doesn't need to be the same. Is your view that blockers and hormones shouldn't be given to people under 16? Or is it that blockers are okay before 16, but hormones are not?

message:
Thanks, a lot of people think that from the spawn of AI --> full robotic machine that can manipulate hardware/software is near instant, when it is a very long process. I think with a lot of simulations we will understand more. 

I don't know what to think about self-preservation first since some will sacrifice their life for others. What is that considered?

I'm seeing the term"AI" being thrown around a lot, but very rarely do people really understand what they're talking about.

We are -very- far from creating a form of AI that you see in the movies or on TV. Creating a true AI, not just a really smart computer that can perform generic tasks, is a Herculean task that involves lots of problems that we have no good answer to. Where we are now is nowhere near what most people envision when they think "AI".

But let's assume we're at that stage. We succeeded in creating a -true- AI, that can truly think for itself. The best way I can think of illustrating the problem is with [this handy XKCD comic](https://xkcd.com/1613/).

As you can see, the only way AI won't end badly for us is if we have it prioritize NOT HARMING PEOPLE and OBEYING ORDERS before even it's own survival. Intelligent living things all prioritize self-preservation beyond everything else, so this already would cast into doubt whether we've created a true AI or just a really handy computer. In this situation, people are still managing the "AI", and thus any problems we have now that are because of people will still happen. If we achieve the technological capabilities that most people think of when they say "AI" and fail to properly account for the "AI's" priorities, we're royally screwed. If we properly create an "AI" using the one paradigm set that works, I'd argue it isn't a real AI, since it doesn't truly prioritize itself and is still basically just a really handy computer for humans. In this case, human related problems will still exist.

message:
First, you state nicotine dependence as if that isn't still a huge drawback to vaping. Everything I stated is because of nicotine dependence. Yes they are better then cigarettes. Far better. However, they aren't risk or consequence free. 

On your second point, most of my experience is with experimental and behavioral alcohol research, but I do have some experience with alcohol pharmacokinetics and effects on neurotransmitters. The main difference between alcohol and other recreational drugs is that alcohols effects are driven by many very different mechanisms. It is not the case that alcohol impacts one or a few neurotransmitters in a straight forward way. Alcohol can do that, but it also does much more. Alcohol has direct effects on neurotransmitters like gaba and serotonin. At the same time, it also significantly impacts the way cells function at the cell membrane and neuronal channel level. In layman's terms alcohol is essentially a poison that seeps into every cell in your body and alters many of their functions. The exact mechanisms can get very complex but this is one of the reasons that at high enough doses alcohol has acute effects on virtually every brain system. 

What does all this mean? It is extremely unlikely that you could come up with a drug that has similar effects while simultaneously mitigating the negative effects on all those brain areas. I can't really emphasize how far away we are from this kind of developmental pharmacology. Most drugs today are basically minor alterations of drugs that we already have. New drugs are often discovered on accident essentially. Very rarely is a drug designed from the ground up with a specific set of brain actions and clinical effects in mind. It's more like, "hey this drug made rats hide less, maybe it will improve anxiety."

With alcohol being a drug with particularly non-specific brain effects and disparate mecahnisms, creating a new alcohol without the negative effects is pretty much a pipe dream at this point.

Some women contribute more to society than some men. Therefore it's illogical to say that *all women* shouldn't be allowed to vote.

Women also pay taxes. Women also work in jobs that support the military, even if it's not their lives that are typically on the line.

You say "it takes almost 3 men to make up for the spending of 1 woman" but this doesn't make sense from a mathematical perspective. For your statement to be true, you'd need 3x more men than women... but that's not the case. What exactly do you mean?

> We work our asses off, just so Women can live on welfare and leach on the system?

Where do you live? I don't know any women on welfare. All the women I know have jobs and pay taxes. Maybe it's different in your country... but I'm pretty sure there are more women NOT on welfare than women on welfare.

> If you do not contribute positively to society, you should not have the right to vote on how to spend the money of other people.

Maybe. But this has nothing to do with women. Most homeless bums are men. You need a better method of determining who contributes to society than simply using gender/sex.

You're dodging my question.

I'm saying that gender is not a good proxy for "contributes to society."

message:
so what about swimming?  should whites have their own Olympics for things like swimming or gymnastics, in which they dominate?  Black people have higher centres of gravity, which makes them faster, while white people and Asians have lower centres of gravity, which makes them faster swimmers.  Asians tend to be physically smaller, however, which puts them at a disadvantage in most sports, so maybe they should have their own as well...the difficulty is determining where we end if we decide to segregate the Olympics.

Most sports in the Olympics were decided by Europeans.  No one in Africa competes in things like rowing or archery, these are largely upper-class white pursuits as well.  Look at the full list of Olympic sports and look at how few African nations actually participate in most of them.

Africans only tend to dominate speed related things.  Body building competitions are dominated by Europeans, as are most things if you look at the medal count.  Kenyans and Ethiopians only dominate at long-distance running because they live at altitude in under-developed areas where they have to run everywhere.  Hypothetically, white people from Colorado or Latin-Americans from the Andes could become just as good, since it's all about endurance if they practised as much as Kenyans do.

It would be completely unfair to remove certain sports just because white people aren't as good at them, you would be removing many African nations' only hope at attaining a medal while ensuring that Europeans win pretty much everything.

Besides, white people already have their own Olympic games, the Winter Olympics! (jk)

Well like you said, although we stopped North Korea, we lost in Vietnam, and both wars were fought with the same justification.

So I can't help but look at that black memorial wall in DC and wonder what they actually died for.  What was the point? Seems like a lot of wasted life fighting someone else's conflict.

the point is that american foreign policy ever since woodrow wilson, give or take a harding or trump, is that freedom threatened in one part of the world threatens it everywhere. that's not justification for blanket military intervention--especially that involves napalming rural villages and executing civilians for suspected collaboration--but revering those who died in the Vietnam war is important not in a personal sense, but for national cohesion. YOU didn't benefit, but, insofar as Americans thought Vietnam was a "good war" in its early stages, it strengthened patriotism collectively. now, that patriotism can still be turned towards good or bad purposes, depending on our leaders, but it needs to be there in the first place. memorial day thus serves a similar purpose as rooting for US athletes in the olympics, or watching us beat the Soviets to the moon.

edit: furthermore, nobody that looks upon the vietnam war memorial has any illusions about the righteousness of our involvement in that war. but that nuance is also important to memorial day. this sort of question, voiced frequently, annually, and correctly, is a recurring prod for our leaders to do better.
